section.title: The Altar of Incense and the Laver
section.sectionRationale: God adds four remaining elements to the tabernacle's furnishing and service: the golden altar of incense before the veil, the atonement money paid at every census, the bronze basin for washing before ministry, and the formulas for the sacred anointing oil and incense, both forbidden for any common use.
section.thesis: God gives instructions for the incense altar, the atonement ransom collected at every census, the basin for washing, and the exclusive formulas for the anointing oil and incense that mark everything and everyone devoted to His service as holy.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the golden altar of incense burned morning and evening; the atonement money paid so no plague comes upon the people; the bronze basin where priests wash before serving; and the sacred anointing oil and incense, reserved for the LORD alone.

media.alt: Smoke rising from a small gold altar
media.caption: Fragrant incense rises before the veil each morning and evening.

verses.text: “You are also to make an altar of acacia wood for the burning of {{incense|incense}}.
It is to be square, a cubit long, a cubit wide, and two cubits high. Its horns must be of one piece.
Overlay with pure gold the top and all the sides and horns, and make a molding of gold around it.
And make two gold rings below the molding on opposite sides to hold the poles used to carry it.
Make the poles of acacia wood and overlay them with gold.
Place the altar in front of the veil that is before the ark of the Testimony, before the mercy seat that is over the Testimony, where I will meet with you.
And Aaron is to burn fragrant incense on it every morning when he tends the lamps.
When Aaron sets up the lamps at twilight, he must burn the incense perpetually before the LORD for the generations to come.
On this altar you must not offer unauthorized incense or a burnt offering or grain offering; nor are you to pour a drink offering on it.

Once a year Aaron shall make atonement on the horns of the altar. Throughout your generations he shall make atonement on it annually with the blood of the sin offering of atonement. The altar is most holy to the LORD.”

units.label: The atonement ransom
units.note: the rich shall not give more, the poor shall not give less

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses,
“When you take a census of the Israelites to number them, each man must pay the LORD a {{ransom|ransom}} for his life when he is counted. Then no plague will come upon them when they are numbered.
Everyone who crosses over to those counted must pay a half shekel, according to the sanctuary shekel, which weighs twenty gerahs. This half shekel is an offering to the LORD.
Everyone twenty years of age or older who crosses over must give this offering to the LORD.
In making the offering to the LORD to atone for your lives, the rich shall not give more than a half shekel, nor shall the poor give less.
Take the atonement money from the Israelites and use it for the service of the Tent of Meeting. It will serve as a memorial for the Israelites before the LORD to make atonement for your lives.”

units.label: The bronze basin
units.note: so that they will not die

verses.text: And the LORD said to Moses,
“You are to make a bronze basin with a bronze stand for washing. Set it between the Tent of Meeting and the altar, and put water in it,
with which Aaron and his sons are to wash their hands and feet.
Whenever they enter the Tent of Meeting or approach the altar to minister by presenting an offering made by fire to the LORD, they must wash with water so that they will not die.
Thus they are to wash their hands and feet so that they will not die; this shall be a permanent statute for Aaron and his descendants for the generations to come.”

units.label: The sacred anointing oil and incense
units.note: holy to the LORD, for no ordinary use

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses,
“Take the finest spices: 500 shekels of liquid myrrh, half that amount, 250 shekels, of fragrant cinnamon, 250 shekels of fragrant cane,
500 shekels of cassia, all according to the sanctuary shekel, and a hin of olive oil.
Prepare from these a sacred {{anointing|anointing oil}}, a fragrant blend, the work of a perfumer; it will be a sacred anointing oil.
Use this oil to anoint the Tent of Meeting, the ark of the Testimony,
the table and all its utensils, the lampstand and its utensils, the altar of incense,
the altar of burnt offering and all its utensils, and the basin with its stand.
You are to consecrate them so that they will be most holy. Whatever touches them shall be holy.
Anoint Aaron and his sons and consecrate them to serve Me as priests.
And you are to tell the Israelites, ‘This will be My sacred anointing oil for the generations to come.
It must not be used to anoint an ordinary man, and you must not make anything like it with the same formula. It is holy, and it must be holy to you.
Anyone who mixes perfume like it or puts it on an outsider shall be cut off from his people.’”
The LORD also said to Moses, “Take fragrant spices, gum resin, onycha, galbanum, and pure frankincense, in equal measures,
and make a fragrant blend of incense, the work of a perfumer, seasoned with salt, pure and holy.
Grind some of it into fine powder and place it in front of the Testimony in the Tent of Meeting, where I will meet with you. It shall be most holy to you.
You are never to use this formula to make incense for yourselves; you shall regard it as holy to the LORD.
Anyone who makes something like it to enjoy its fragrance shall be cut off from his people.”

incense.gloss: Fragrant incense, burned continually on the golden altar before the veil. Its rising smoke became a common biblical picture for the prayers of God's people ascending before Him, Psalm 141:2, Revelation 8:3-4.

ransom.gloss: A ransom or price paid to cover a life, related to the word for atonement. Every Israelite counted in a census paid the same amount, rich or poor, since no one's life could be ransomed for more or less than another's before God.

anointing.gloss: The sacred anointing oil, a specific fragrant blend reserved exclusively for consecrating the tabernacle, its furnishings, and its priests. Forbidden for any ordinary use, it marked whatever it touched as set apart wholly for the LORD's service.

basic.context: God gives four remaining instructions for the tabernacle's service: a golden altar for incense burned morning and evening before the veil, a small ransom every Israelite pays at a census, a bronze basin where priests wash before ministering, and the exact, exclusive formulas for the anointing oil and incense used only for God's service.
basic.hermeneutics: These are literal, practical instructions for Israel's ongoing worship, not primarily symbols to decode. Each element serves a real function, atonement, cleansing, consecration, while also carrying meaning the rest of Scripture develops, such as incense representing prayer.
basic.originalLanguages: The qetoret, incense, rose continually before the veil, and later Scripture uses this image directly for prayer ascending to God. Every Israelite counted paid the same kopher, ransom, since atonement for a life could not be bought more cheaply or dearly depending on wealth. The shemen mishchat qodesh, sacred anointing oil, was reserved exclusively for God's service, forbidden on pain of being cut off from Israel if copied for common use.
basic.typology: The rising incense before the veil pictures the prayers of God's people ascending to Him, fulfilled as believers now pray boldly through Christ. The equal ransom paid by rich and poor alike anticipates the equal, priceless ransom Christ pays for all who trust Him, regardless of status.
basic.christ: Revelation pictures the prayers of the saints rising like incense before God's throne, Revelation 8:3-4, an image rooted directly in this altar. Paul calls Christ's death a ransom for all, 1 Timothy 2:6, echoing this chapter's ransom that could not be increased or decreased by human wealth or merit.
basic.god: God requires a ransom for every life numbered, showing that even being counted among His people carries the weight of sin needing atonement. Yet He fixes that ransom at the same small price for rich and poor alike, revealing that before Him, no one's worth or need for atonement outweighs another's.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 141:2 asks that prayer be counted as incense before God. Revelation 8:3-4 pictures the prayers of the saints rising with incense before God's throne. 1 Timothy 2:6 calls Christ's death a ransom for all people.
basic.oneStory: God ordains incense that rises continually before His presence, a ransom paid equally by every Israelite, and an anointing oil reserved exclusively for His service. Together they anticipate Christ, whose death ransoms all people equally and through whom believers' prayers now rise continually before God like fragrant incense.

context.literary: This chapter gathers four remaining pieces of tabernacle instruction, the incense altar, the census ransom, the washing basin, and the two exclusive formulas, closing out the extended section that began in chapter 25 before the narrative resumes with the calling of the craftsmen in chapter 31.
context.historical: The census ransom described here is later carried out in Numbers 1, where every man twenty and older is counted and pays the same half shekel, funding the tabernacle's service and functioning as a perpetual memorial before the LORD.
context.canonical: The image of incense as prayer becomes deeply embedded in Israel's worship, especially the Psalms, and reappears transformed in Revelation's vision of the heavenly altar, while the exclusivity of the anointing oil underscores the seriousness of anything claiming to represent God's own consecrating presence.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To complete the tabernacle's furnishing and service with instructions that emphasize continual prayer-like worship, atonement required of every Israelite regardless of status, and the strict exclusivity of whatever is set apart for God's own use.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage prescribes specific old covenant practices, the census ransom, the exact oil and incense formulas, for Israel's worship; it does not prescribe these formulas or a literal census tax for the church today. Its abiding truth is that approaching God requires atonement and that what is holy to Him must not be treated as common.

debates.question: Why does God require a ransom at a census specifically, when censuses themselves were not inherently sinful?

views.label: Numbering as a form of self-reliant pride
views.case: Many interpreters connect this to 2 Samuel 24, where David's later census, taken without collecting this ransom, brings a plague, suggesting that counting God's people could tempt leaders to trust in numbers and military strength rather than God, requiring an accompanying act of atonement to guard against that pride.

views.label: A general reminder that every life belongs to God
views.case: Others see the ransom less as addressing a specific sin in counting and more as a broader, built-in reminder that every Israelite's life, simply by being reckoned as part of the covenant community, ultimately belongs to and is redeemed by God, regardless of the specific occasion.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the ransom teaches that human life and its accounting before God are never neutral; atonement and humility before God's ownership of His people are required, whatever the precise occasion for numbering.

study.originalLanguages: The qetoret rises continually before the veil where God meets with Moses, an image later Scripture applies directly to prayer, Psalm 141:2. The kopher, ransom, is fixed at the same half shekel for every Israelite twenty and older, rich or poor, since no life could be ransomed for more or less before a holy God. The shemen mishchat qodesh, sacred anointing oil, and the incense of verses 34 through 38 are both explicitly forbidden for any common imitation, on pain of being cut off from Israel.

typology.type: Incense rising continually before the veil
typology.fulfillment: The prayers of God's people, now offered continually through Christ, pictured rising before God's throne, Revelation 8:3-4.

typology.type: The equal ransom paid by every Israelite, rich or poor
typology.fulfillment: Christ's ransom for all people, offered equally regardless of human status or wealth, 1 Timothy 2:6.

study.christ: Paul writes that Christ Jesus 'gave Himself as a ransom for all,' 1 Timothy 2:6, using the same conceptual ground as the equal ransom required of every Israelite here, since before God no one's atonement can be bought more cheaply or dearly. The continually rising incense before God's presence anticipates believers' access to pray without ceasing through the one Mediator, Hebrews 4:16.
study.god: God requires atonement even for the simple act of being numbered among His people, showing that nothing about approaching Him is casual or automatic. Yet He fixes that atonement at an equal, modest price for every Israelite, revealing that His mercy toward the poor is as full as toward the rich, and that His holiness, symbolized in the exclusive oil and incense, cannot be imitated or commodified by human hands.

crossReferences.note: May my prayer be set before You like incense, drawing directly on this altar's imagery.
Pictures the prayers of the saints rising with incense before God's throne.
Christ gave Himself as a ransom for all, echoing this chapter's equal atonement price.

study.oneStory: God ordains a continually burning incense before His presence, an equal ransom paid by every Israelite counted among His people, and an anointing oil reserved exclusively for His service. All of it anticipates Christ, whose death ransoms people of every status equally and through whom believers' prayers now rise before God without ceasing.

quiz.q: When was Aaron to burn the fragrant incense on the golden altar?

options.text: Every morning and at twilight, perpetually before the LORD.
options.note: Verses 7 to 8 specify this continual, twice daily pattern.

options.text: Only once a year, on the Day of Atonement.
options.note: The annual atonement on the altar's horns, verse 10, was separate from the regular morning and evening incense burning.

options.text: Whenever an individual worshiper requested it.
options.note: The incense schedule was fixed by God, tied to the tending of the lamps, not left to individual request.

options.text: No regular schedule is given.
options.note: Verses 7 to 8 explicitly establish a morning and evening pattern.

quiz.q: How much was each Israelite required to pay as atonement money at a census, according to verse 15?

options.text: A half shekel, the same amount for rich and poor alike.
options.note: Verse 15 states explicitly that the rich shall not give more and the poor shall not give less.

options.text: An amount proportional to each person's wealth.
options.note: The text explicitly rejects a wealth-based sliding scale; the amount was fixed and equal.

options.text: A full shekel from every household.
options.note: The specified amount was a half shekel per individual, not a full shekel per household.

options.text: No payment was required.
options.note: Verses 12 through 15 explicitly require this atonement payment.

quiz.q: Why did Aaron and his sons need to wash their hands and feet at the bronze basin?

options.text: So that they would not die when entering the Tent of Meeting or approaching the altar.
options.note: Verses 20 to 21 give this exact, sober reason twice.

options.text: Simply as a matter of personal hygiene.
options.note: While washing has hygienic benefit, the text specifically frames this as a matter of life and death before God, not mere hygiene.

options.text: To prepare their appearance before the people.
options.note: The stated purpose concerns approaching God at the altar and tent, not their appearance before the congregation.

options.text: No reason is given for the washing.
options.note: Verses 20 to 21 explicitly state the reason: so that they will not die.

quiz.q: What was forbidden regarding the sacred anointing oil, according to verses 32 to 33?

options.text: It must not be used to anoint an ordinary man, nor could anyone make anything like it with the same formula.
options.note: Verses 32 to 33 explicitly state this prohibition, with a penalty of being cut off from the people.

options.text: It could be freely sold to any Israelite family.
options.note: The text explicitly prohibits common use or replication of the oil, not permits its sale.

options.text: It could be used, but only by the wealthy.
options.note: The prohibition applies to any ordinary use regardless of wealth, not merely a restriction based on economic status.

options.text: No restrictions were placed on the oil's use.
options.note: Verses 32 to 33 explicitly restrict the oil's use to sacred purposes alone.

quiz.q: What penalty is given for making incense like the sacred formula simply to enjoy its fragrance?

options.text: Being cut off from his people.
options.note: Verse 38 states this exact penalty.

options.text: A fine paid in silver.
options.note: The stated penalty is being cut off from the community, not a monetary fine.

options.text: Temporary exclusion from the camp for seven days.
options.note: The text specifies being cut off from the people, a far more severe and permanent consequence than a temporary exclusion.

options.text: No penalty is mentioned.
options.note: Verse 38 explicitly states the penalty of being cut off from the people.

questions: Incense rose before God morning and evening without fail, later pictured as the prayers of His people. How consistent is your own pattern of prayer, morning and evening, compared to this continual rhythm?

The atonement ransom was the same for the richest and poorest Israelite alike. Where might you be tempted to think your standing before God depends on what you have achieved or accumulated, rather than on Christ's equal ransom for all?
Aaron and his sons had to wash before every act of service, not just once at ordination. What ongoing, regular practices of confession and cleansing do you build into your own walk with God?
The anointing oil and incense were reserved exclusively for God, never to be copied for ordinary enjoyment. Where do you see the sacred and the ordinary blurred together in your own life or worship?

God required atonement simply for the Israelites being counted among His people. How does remembering that even being numbered among God's people required atonement shape your sense of what it cost for you to belong to Him?

translationNotes.note: Once a year Aaron shall make atonement on the horns of the altar refers to the Day of Atonement ritual detailed later in Leviticus 16:18-19, distinct from the daily incense offering described in the earlier verses of this chapter.
The census ransom described here is later carried out in Numbers 1:1-3, 46, where the total silver collected corresponds to the exact half shekel per counted man commanded in this passage.

Onycha and galbanum are aromatic substances whose precise ancient identity is debated among scholars, though their general character as fragrant resins used in perfumery is well attested in the ancient Near East.
